reasoning: The question asks if the number of federal employees will drop by more than 250,000 from January 2025 to any point before January 2026, based on Kalshi's resolution criteria using FRED data (typically All Employees, Federal). A drop of 250,000 in a single year would be historically unprecedented outside of census years (like 2020 to 2021) or major demobilizations. The current civilian federal workforce is around 3.0 million. Cutting 250k would be ~8% of the federal workforce. While the rhetoric from Trump and DOGE (Elon Musk/Vivek Ramaswamy) is aggressive, actually terminating 250,000 employees requires navigating civil service protections (which take time to dismantle, even if Schedule F is reinstated), overcoming union pushback/legal injunctions, and likely waiting for congressional action or attrition. The typical annual attrition is around 6-8%, meaning a complete hiring freeze could theoretically achieve 150-200k in one year if zero hiring occurred, but practical operations necessitate some hiring, and many defense/security areas will probably be exempt. In late 2024 forecasting markets, the odds of an actual 250k+ reduction in federal headcount within the first year (2025) are priced relatively low (around 10-15%). While a hiring freeze and aggressive early retirement push might reduce headcount by 50-100k, reaching >250k in a single calendar year is very challenging. Therefore, the probability is placed at 10%.
